CLARITY.justification: The paper has a clear overall structure (discussion of psychological background, two-class metric proposal, detailed prerequisite skills, annotation procedure, and discussion) so the logical flow is present. However, the provided text contains many presentation problems — repeated/duplicated sentences and paragraphs, LaTeX/footnote artifacts, and awkwardly formatted elements (e.g., Table 6, repeated references to figures) — that make some parts hard to follow. The skills list and annotation procedure are fairly well defined, but definitions and the treatment of ambiguous cases (e.g., “solvable” questions) are only partly clarified. Overall the writing is adequate but the manuscript needs careful copy-editing to remove repetitions, fix formatting, and resolve ambiguous phrasing.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper combines and refines existing theories and resources—adopting McNamara and Magliano’s two-aspect view of comprehension and refining Sugawara et al.’s skills into thirteen prerequisite skills (Section 3.1) while using Vajjala & Meurers (2012) readability features—to produce a dual-class evaluation framework and annotations across six RC datasets (Section 4). These contributions are useful and clearly differentiated from prior work (they explicitly cite and build on Kintsch, Sugawara, and Vajjala), but they are largely an organized synthesis and incremental refinement rather than a fundamentally new method or problem formulation. The techniques (skill reorganization and dataset annotation) are non-trivial application work but not highly novel, so the contribution is moderately novel and informative to experts.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: The paper is squarely within the scope of top NLP venues: it proposes diagnostic evaluation metrics for reading comprehension (two classes: prerequisite skills and readability), grounds them in established cognitive theories (Kintsch; McNamara & Magliano), and applies them via annotation and corpus analysis to six major RC datasets (e.g., SQuAD, MCTest, MS MARCO, QA4MRE). The methodology (manual annotation, use of established linguistic/readability features from Vajjala & Meurers, dataset-level analyses and correlations) and the audience (RC/dataset evaluation researchers) match expectations for a top-tier NLP conference; the problem formulation and positioning are appropriate and relevant to the community.

SUBSTANCE.justification: The paper performs a multi-dataset annotation and analysis (six RC datasets; annotation procedure described in Section 4 and Table 6 reports sentence counts/distances) and grounds metrics in prior work (psychology: Kintsch/McNamara; entailment literature; readability features from Vajjala & Meurers). It discusses limitations and failure modes (Section 6: annotation issues, disagreements about ‘nonsense’, and inability to evaluate sentence-selection competence). However, no ablation or sensitivity analyses are presented, key reproducibility details are missing from the provided text (no inter-annotator agreement numbers, exact sample sizes per dataset, or full annotation guidelines), and some experimental depth/statistical analysis appears lacking to fully support broader claims.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper presents a plausible evaluation framework (two metric classes, refined 13 prerequisite skills) and reports dataset annotations and summary statistics (e.g., Table 6), but key methodological details and quantitative validation are missing. The annotation protocol omits inter-annotator agreement, aggregation rules, and sample sizes (Section 4.1 and Table 6), and the sentence-distance definition in Appendix B is ambiguous and algorithmically unclear. There are no reported statistical tests or rigorous experiments demonstrating that number of skills correlates with difficulty (the central assumption cited from Sugawara et al. is not empirically validated here), though limitations are acknowledged (Section 6). These gaps suggest some concerns but not fatal flaws in the overall approach.

IMPACT.justification: The paper tackles an important and practical problem—characterizing RC dataset difficulty—by proposing two evaluation classes grounded in cognitive theory (prerequisite skills, refined to 13 types, and readability features from Vajjala & Meurers) and annotating six existing RC datasets. Its empirical finding that readability does not directly predict question difficulty and the proposed uses (dataset design, curriculum/transfer learning) can change how researchers construct and select benchmarks and guide model development. The work is likely to be built upon by dataset creators and evaluators and opens clear follow-up directions (better metrics, dataset generation), though it stays within the RC/dataset-analysis subfield rather than being transformational across AI.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: The paper situates its metrics against a broad set of prior work (e.g., Kintsch 1988/1993, McNamara & Magliano 2009, Sugawara et al. 2017, Vajjala & Meurers 2012) and explicitly refines Sugawara’s skills while applying the metrics to six existing RC datasets (Section 4 and Table 6). Comparisons appear fair and consistent: the same annotation procedure was applied across datasets and choices to exclude problematic corpora (CNN/Daily Mail, CBTest) are justified (Chen et al., 2016; task formulation). The paper acknowledges limitations (e.g., handling “no answer” questions and competence of sentence selection) and explains differences from prior classifications, but it does not present exhaustive quantitative benchmarking against alternative dataset-quality metrics, hence a minor omission.
